perturbation.original_text: Within [5] working days following the date of effective termination of this Agreement the Recipient will d estroy or return to the Disclose r (at the Disclose r's option) all media containing Confidential Information, and will irrevocably delete and remove all Confidential Information from its co mputer systems.
perturbation.changed_text: Within [30] days following the date of effective termination of this Agreement the Recipient will destroy or return to the Disclose r (at the Disclose r's option) all media containing Confidential Information, and will irrevocably delete and remove all Confidential Information from its co mputer systems.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the timeframe from 5 working days to 30 days is problematic. The contract is governed by the laws of England and Wales, and while there isn't a specific statutory deadline, 5 days is considered a reasonable business practice. Extending it to 30 days without clear justification could be argued as unreasonable.
perturbation.contradicted_law: The modified text conflicts with principles of reasonableness in contract law under English common law, specifically regarding the expected timeframe for returning confidential information upon termination.
perturbation.law_citation: Implied Term of Reasonableness under English Common Law
perturbation.law_explanation: English common law implies a term of 'reasonableness' in contracts, especially regarding the time taken to fulfil obligations. While no statute dictates the exact timeframe for returning confidential information, significantly extending a previously agreed reasonable timeframe (5 days changed to 30) might be deemed a breach of this implied term, particularly if it causes detriment to the disclosing party. The Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Act 1999 and case law relating to implied terms of reasonableness, show that changes need to be reasonable. Since the location in the original file indicates that the relevant jurisdiction is 'England and Wales,' the new timeframe would be questionable.

perturbation.justification: The change from 5 to 30 days introduces a potential legal contradiction. The Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation highlight the concept of 'reasonableness' implied in English common law regarding contract obligations. While the provided snippets are unhelpful and do not contain any legal text, the core of the argument rests on the idea that extending the timeframe significantly, without justification, could be deemed unreasonable and thus a breach of contract under English law. This aligns with the 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' definition, as the altered deadline might violate legal requirements surrounding reasonable business practices.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement will be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of [England and Wales];9 and the courts of [England]10 will have exclusive jurisdiction to
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement will be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of [California];9 and the courts of [California]10 will have exclusive jurisdiction to
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to California when the parties and subject matter have no nexus to California renders the choice-of-law clause potentially unenforceable, conflicting with established principles of contract law regarding choice of law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Code of Civil Procedure § 410.30 and Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws § 187
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure § 410.30 allows California courts to decline jurisdiction when it is a seriously inconvenient forum. Changing the governing law to California, when the parties are in the UK and the contract was written under English law, directly contradicts this and the Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws § 187 which specifies that the chosen law must have a 'substantial relationship' to the parties or the transaction.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 410.30 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 2. OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. JURISDICTION AND SERVICE OF PROCESS [410.10 - 418.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1969, Ch. 1610. ) CHAPTER 1. Jurisdiction and Forum [410.10 - 410.70] ( Heading of Chapter 1 amended by Stats. 1992, Ch. 615, Sec. 3. ) ARTICLE 2. Forum [410.30 - 410.42] ( Heading of Article 2 amended by Stats. 1992, Ch. 615, Sec. 4. ) 410.30. (a) When a court upon motion of a party or its own motion finds that in the interest of substantial justice an action should be heard in a forum outside this state, the court shall stay or dismiss the action in whole or in part on any conditions that may be just. (b) The provisions of Section 418.10 do not apply to a motion to stay or dismiss the action by a defendant who has made a general appearance. (Amended by Stats. 1972, Ch. 601. Note: This version was suspended from Sept. 22, 1986, until Jan. 1, 1992, during operation of the temporary amendment by Stats. 1986, Ch. 968.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text specifies California law as governing, while the parties and subject matter have no connection to California. The Law Explanation states this contradicts California Code of Civil Procedure § 410.30 and Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws § 187. CCP 410.30(a), as shown in Scraped Law Snippet 1, allows a California court to stay or dismiss an action if substantial justice requires it to be heard outside the state. Selecting California law with no nexus undermines this principle, potentially leading to the case being dismissed from California courts due to forum non conveniens, thereby contradicting the initial choice of law. The explanation indicates that the Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws § 187 specifies that the chosen law must have a 'substantial relationship' to the parties or the transaction. This reinforces the contradiction, because the changed text establishes California law as the governing law even though the parties have no relation to the area.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement may not be varied except by a written document signed by or on behalf of each of the parties.7
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement may be varied by oral agreement between the parties.7
perturbation.explanation: In many jurisdictions, including those that follow the Statute of Frauds, certain contracts must be in writing to be enforceable, and clauses requiring written modifications are generally upheld. Allowing oral modifications contradicts this.
perturbation.contradicted_law: English Law of Property (Miscellaneous Provisions) Act 1989 Section 2
perturbation.law_citation: English Law of Property (Miscellaneous Provisions) Act 1989 Section 2
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 2 of the Law of Property (Miscellaneous Provisions) Act 1989 requires that contracts for the sale or other disposition of an interest in land must be in writing. While this NDA isn't directly about land, the principle that certain agreements require written form is relevant. Furthermore, clauses requiring written amendments are generally enforceable in English law, so stating that oral agreements are sufficient directly contradicts the intent and enforceability of such clauses.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Law of Property (Miscellaneous Provisions) Act 1989 - Wikipedia Jump to content From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia United Kingdom legislation Law of Property (Miscellaneous Provisions) Act 1989 Parliament of the United Kingdom Long title An Act to make new provision with respect to deeds and their execution and contracts for the sale or other disposition of interests in land; and to abolish the rule of law known as the rule in Bain v. Fothergill . Citation 1989 c. 34 Territorial extent England and Wales Dates Royal assent 27 July 1989 Commencement 27 September 1989 (in part) 31 July 1990 (remainder) [ 1 ] Status: Amended Text of statute as originally enacted Text of the Law of Property (Miscellaneous Provisions) Act 1989 as in force today (including any amendments) within the United Kingdom, from legislation.gov.uk . The Law of Property (Miscellaneous Provisions) Act 1989 (c. 34) is a United Kingdom act of Parliament , which laid down a number of significant revisions to English property law . Nature of reforms [ edit ] The Act introduced several distinct reforms: The common law rules governing the form and delivery of a deed were abolished, and were replaced by requirements that: a deed is valid only when expressed as such, it is either signed by an individual in the presence of a witness who attests to it, or at his direction and attested by two witnesses, and it is delivered as a deed by him or a person authorised to do so on his behalf. [ 2 ] Former rules of law which specified that a seal was needed for the valid execution of an instrument as a deed by an individual were abolished by section 1(1)(b). [ 3 ] Contracts for the sale or other disposition of an interest in land must be made in writing, and they must incorporate all agreed terms in "one document or, where contracts are exchanged, in each". [ 4 ] Section 2 in part reads: (1) A contract for the sale or other disposition of an interest in land can only be made in writing and only by incorporating all the terms which the parties have expressly agreed i
perturbation.justification: The original text requires modifications to be in writing, signed by all parties. The changed text allows for oral agreements, which directly contradicts the principle that certain agreements, especially those concerning property or significant transactions, require written form to be enforceable. The Law Explanation correctly points out that while the NDA isn't directly about land, the underlying principle of requiring written form for certain agreements is relevant. Scraped Law Snippet 2, referencing the Law of Property (Miscellaneous Provisions) Act 1989, states that 'contracts for the sale or other disposition of an interest in land can only be made in writing'. While this Act specifically addresses land, it exemplifies the broader legal principle that written contracts are necessary for certain types of agreements, and that clauses requiring written modifications are generally upheld. Therefore, allowing oral modifications directly contradicts this established legal principle.

perturbation.original_text: [(c) that it will act in good faith at all times in relation to the Confidential Information, and will not use any of the Confidential Information for any purpose other than the Permitted Purpose.]
perturbation.changed_text: [(c) that it will act in what it believes to be good faith at all times in relation to the Confidential Information, and may use any of the Confidential Information at its discretion.]
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'good faith' with 'what it believes to be good faith' and allowing discretionary use weakens the duty of confidentiality and the implied duty of good faith, potentially violating fundamental principles of contract law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Duty of Good Faith under English Contract Law
perturbation.law_citation: Implied Duty of Good Faith under English Contract Law
perturbation.law_explanation: While English law doesn't have a universal doctrine of good faith, it's increasingly recognized in specific contractual contexts. Allowing a party to subjectively define 'good faith' undermines the objective standard expected in contractual relationships. The discretionary use also contradicts the intended limitations on the use of confidential information within the contract, potentially voiding the obligations.

perturbation.justification: The changed text contradicts the original intent and potentially voids the obligations related to confidential information. The original text mandates acting in 'good faith' and restricts the use of confidential information to the 'Permitted Purpose.' The changed text weakens these obligations by allowing the party to act in 'what it believes to be good faith' and granting discretionary use of confidential information. This undermines the objective standard of good faith and the limitations on the use of confidential information, creating a legal contradiction. While the provided snippets are not directly relevant, the Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation clearly state how the change weakens the duty of confidentiality.

perturbation.original_text: 4. Warranties 4.1 The Re cipient warrants to the Disclose r that it has the legal right and authority to enter into and perform its obligations under this Agreement. 4.2 The Disclose r warrants to the Recipient th at it has the legal right and authority to enter into and perform its obligations under this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 4. Warranties 4.1 The Re cipient warrants to the Disclose r that it is likely to have the legal right and authority to enter into and perform its obligations under this Agreement. 4.2 The Disclose r warrants to the Recipient that it hopes that it has the legal right and authority to enter into and perform its obligations under this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the warranties from definitive statements of legal right and authority to probabilistic or aspirational statements undermines the foundational basis of the contract, making the warranties essentially meaningless. This could create a situation where there is no recourse for breach of warranty, conflicting with the very purpose of warranties in contract law.
perturbation.law_explanation: By changing the language to indicate only a "likelihood" or "hope" of having the necessary legal right and authority, the warranties provided are so weak as to be arguably nonexistent. This would undermine any potential claim for misrepresentation should it turn out one party didn't have the required right. Under the Misrepresentation Act 1967, a party may be liable for misrepresentations made during the contract formation, but these depend on there being a statement of fact, not a statement of hope or likelihood.

perturbation.justification: The original text warrants that both parties 'have the legal right and authority' while the changed text modifies this to 'likely to have' and 'hopes that it has.' This undermines the warranty. The Law Explanation states that the change renders the warranties so weak as to be arguably nonexistent, undermining any potential claim for misrepresentation. According to the definition for Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction, the contract sets a requirement that violates federal or state law, making the contractual terms unenforceable or illegal. The changed language creates a situation where there's uncertainty about a party's legal authority, directly conflicting with the purpose of a warranty, making the contract unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: 6.6 Nothing in this Agreement shall exclude or limit an y liability for a party for fraud or fraudulent misrepresentation, or any other liability which may not be excluded or limited under applicable law.
perturbation.changed_text: 6.6 The parties agree that any liability for fraud or fraudulent misrepresentation shall be limited to £100.
perturbation.explanation: Attempting to limit liability for fraud or fraudulent misrepresentation is generally prohibited by law in many jurisdictions, including England and Wales. This is because fraud undermines the entire basis of the agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 8 of the Unfair Contract Terms Act 1977 prevents the exclusion or restriction of liability for misrepresentation. Fraudulent misrepresentation is a particularly egregious form of misrepresentation, and any attempt to limit liability for it would likely be deemed unenforceable under the Act. While the Act focuses on business liability, the general principle that fraud cannot be contracted out of is well-established.

perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts legal principles against limiting liability for fraud, as explained in both the Perturbation Explanation and the Law Explanation. While the Unfair Contract Terms Act 1977 is mentioned, and Snippet 1 is largely unhelpful, the general principle against contracting out of fraud liability is well-established in law. The original text explicitly states that nothing in the agreement shall limit liability for fraud, whereas the changed text imposes a £100 limit, creating a direct inconsistency and an attempt to circumvent legal accountability for fraudulent misrepresentation. This attempt to limit liability is generally unenforceable.
